state.name: Connecticut
state.agency: Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, Wildlife Division
state.peak_intrusion_season: March through May during spring denning, and again from late September through November as animals prepare for winter
state.climate_factor: Connecticut's cold winters push rodents, bats, and squirrels into attics aggressively, while warm humid summers drive heavy raccoon and skunk activity in suburban yards
state.permit_note: Connecticut requires a Nuisance Wildlife Control Operator (NWCO) permit for commercial trapping, and bears, bobcats, and migratory birds have additional state and federal handling restrictions
state.county_government_note: Connecticut formally dissolved county government in 1960, but the eight counties remain as geographic and legal designations

Fairfield County.region: southwestern Connecticut along Long Island Sound, bordering New York
Fairfield County.nearest_metro: New York City (Stamford is about 35 miles from Manhattan)
Fairfield County.habitat_type: dense suburban Gold Coast along the Sound, with wooded interior towns to the north
Fairfield County.regional_wildlife: coyote populations are unusually dense in the affluent suburban towns, and black bear sightings have steadily increased in northern Fairfield County since 2015
Fairfield County.absent_or_rare: moose are uncommon this far south in Connecticut

Hartford County.region: central Connecticut along the Connecticut River valley
Hartford County.nearest_metro: Hartford is itself the regional metro center
Hartford County.habitat_type: the urban Hartford core, dense suburbs throughout the river valley, and rocky traprock ridges to the west
Hartford County.regional_wildlife: fisher cats are well-established across the suburban towns, and bear activity in the western Hartford County hills now produces frequent residential calls
Hartford County.absent_or_rare: porcupines are scarce this far south of their core New England range

Litchfield County.region: northwestern Connecticut, the most rural and mountainous part of the state
Litchfield County.nearest_metro: Hartford (about 30 miles east) and Waterbury (about 20 miles south)
Litchfield County.habitat_type: wooded hills and small mountains, scattered farmland, and lake communities — the most forested county in the state
Litchfield County.regional_wildlife: Litchfield County has the highest black bear density in Connecticut, and bear-proofing services are in heavy demand spring through fall
Litchfield County.absent_or_rare: this is the only Connecticut county where moose sightings are regularly reported

Middlesex County.region: south-central Connecticut along the lower Connecticut River and Long Island Sound
Middlesex County.nearest_metro: Hartford (about 20 miles north) and New Haven (about 25 miles west)
Middlesex County.habitat_type: river towns, small Sound-facing shoreline communities, and significant inland forest
Middlesex County.regional_wildlife: the lower Connecticut River corridor concentrates muskrats, beavers, and waterfowl issues, and bat activity in older shoreline homes is a recurring summer problem
Middlesex County.absent_or_rare: black bears are increasing here but still less common than in the northwest

New Haven County.county_seat: historically New Haven (county government dissolved in 1960)
New Haven County.region: south-central Connecticut along Long Island Sound
New Haven County.nearest_metro: New Haven is the regional center, with New York City about 80 miles southwest
New Haven County.habitat_type: the urban cores of New Haven and Waterbury, dense suburbs, and the rocky traprock ridges that run through the county
New Haven County.regional_wildlife: rat populations are heavy in the older neighborhoods of New Haven and Waterbury, and coyotes are now established in nearly every suburban town
New Haven County.absent_or_rare: moose and porcupines are rare in this densely populated part of the state

New London County.county_seat: historically New London (county government dissolved in 1960)
New London County.region: southeastern Connecticut along Long Island Sound and the Rhode Island border
New London County.nearest_metro: Providence, RI (about 50 miles east) and Hartford (about 50 miles northwest)
New London County.habitat_type: shoreline towns, the large Pachaug forest in the north, and rural farming communities in between
New London County.regional_wildlife: Pachaug State Forest and the surrounding towns have growing black bear and bobcat populations, and waterfront properties along the Mystic and Thames rivers see frequent otter and beaver issues
New London County.absent_or_rare: moose are rare but occasionally wander down from northern New England

Tolland County.region: north-central and northeastern Connecticut, including the area around UConn
Tolland County.habitat_type: rolling wooded hills, university and lake-area communities, and small dairy farms
Tolland County.regional_wildlife: bear activity in residential areas around Tolland and Stafford has grown sharply, and university-area neighborhoods in Mansfield see heavy raccoon and skunk pressure
Tolland County.absent_or_rare: porcupines occur but are uncommon, and moose sightings are rare

Windham County.region: northeastern Connecticut, the 'Quiet Corner,' bordering Massachusetts and Rhode Island
Windham County.nearest_metro: Worcester, MA (about 30 miles northeast) and Hartford (about 35 miles west)
Windham County.habitat_type: the most rural region in the state, with extensive forest, hill farms, and small mill villages
Windham County.regional_wildlife: Windham County's rural character means bear, fisher, and bobcat encounters are routine, and rural barns and outbuildings often host bat colonies and raccoon families
Windham County.absent_or_rare: moose are very occasionally reported, mostly along the Massachusetts border
